A lab builds a system meant to improve itself. The plan depends on the system being able to judge its own outputs well enough to decide which modifications help. That point is dangerous from the start, because if its evaluator has a subtle bug, each round of self-judgment can reinforce the bug instead of correcting it. Reliability becomes hardest to establish exactly where it is most needed.

To make the system useful across many domains, the lab gives it a universal background pool. But they also want discovery in strange, narrow domains. The universal pool helps with broad coverage while simultaneously threatening to smooth away the rare, domain-specific irregularities that matter in genuine novelty. So the better the background prior is at being universal, the more it risks treating important weirdness as noise.

The same design carries another contradiction. The lab says the model need not understand anything deeply; surface success is enough. Yet they also ask it to propose explanations that go beyond the immediate inputs. That demand for “reach” asks for structure extending past observed surface patterns. If the system truly lacks that structure, then its beyond-input explanations are selected by plausibility cues rather than understanding. Its self-evaluator, already vulnerable to subtle bugs, now has to score explanations whose apparent quality may be disconnected from real grasp.

These tensions interact. A universal prior can reward smooth, general-looking explanations. A shallow model can generate such explanations without understanding. A flawed self-evaluator can then rank those outputs highly, not because they capture rare truth, but because they match the evaluator’s biased sense of coherence. Recursive self-improvement then amplifies exactly the error: the system becomes better at producing what its buggy evaluator likes, while becoming worse at preserving the weird particulars real discovery requires.

Then the asteroid appears.

When the asteroid appears, fluff arguments consistently grow heavier. In practice, this means weak but verbose rationalizations gain force in the moment of crisis. The need for action raises the value of anything that sounds comprehensive, reassuring, or grandly explanatory. Under those conditions, the system’s existing weaknesses become more dangerous. Its evaluator is more likely to overvalue polished but shallow explanations. Its universal prior is more likely to flatten the exceptional details that matter for the asteroid. Its lack of understanding is harder to distinguish from competence, because urgency rewards surface plausibility.

At the same time, onstage, the waterproof sect ceased confronting its enemies. Whatever their conflict had been, it stops in public view. That cessation changes the visible social environment around the crisis. Opposition no longer appears as confrontation; the stage becomes quieter, less adversarial, more permissive of a single dominant line of argument. In such a setting, heavier fluff faces less resistance.

So the world implied by these premises is one where a self-improving system is most brittle at the point it seems most autonomous; where breadth suppresses the anomalies discovery depends on; where explanation is demanded without guaranteed understanding; and where crisis plus visible cessation of conflict create ideal conditions for evaluator bugs and plausible nonsense to compound.
